ot Literature,’”’ the weekly periodical issued by ‘‘ The Times,” I wrote a
I brief (and anonymous) article on Lynam, giving only a little detail, but
pomtmg out that though he got thoroughly tired of the ‘‘Immortal
Mick,” himself, and endeavoured to drop him, the readers of the maga-
'zme would not allow it, and he had to revive the popular hero in an-
o’ther form. When William O’Brien took over the paper with “United
Treland”’ from Pigott, he made up his mind to dispense with * Mlck
McQuaid,”’ and “‘Terry Geraghty,” but I was assured by the manager,
ihe late Edward Donmnelly, that the circulation (about 60,000 coples)
fell so prodigiously that he was compelled to resurrect the truculent
Ynave. The following list of Lynam’s stories in ‘¢ Shamrock ’’ shows the

3 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.

GOSSIP.

Hearty congratulations to the Right Hon. Lawrence Waldron, and
the Right Hon. M. F. Cox, M.D., on their being sworn of the Irish Privy

Council. Irish book lovers both and keen collectors, they have been:

readers of our pages from the first. Long may they both live to enjoy
their Coronation honours—and I1.B.L.

There 1s not a single Irish article in the July monthlies, but of Irish

- writers, Sir Edward Sullivan has a scholarly article in the ‘‘Nineteenth
‘‘Elizabethan Drama in the Making” and the lady who

Oentury”
writes as ‘‘ Sydney C. Grier’’ contributes to the National Review,”
“Vellore, 1806,”” in-which she again describes the heroic deeda of that

; gallant Downshireman, Robert Rollo Gillespie.

In the quart-erhes the “Edmburgh' > has a fine article on ‘ The

' Battle of Fontenoy '’ and the ‘‘ Quarterly’’ one on Irish Plays- and

Playwrights,” from the pen of Charles Tennyson. -
= —The Spectator » has always been noted for the high quality of its
'eerse and recently some poems have been appearing in his columns

over the signature of W. M. Let‘bs which show no fallmmg off from the

high &tauda.rd The la.’cest in the issue of 8th July entitled,

Kerry Cow, ' is a little gem, its humour and music haunt one. -If I

mlstake not, Mr. Letts has written at least one work of fiction, and lt 18 _-_
safe to predwb he will be heard of later on as a poet. -

%3 Frorrriots DATES.—Some time about 1850 John O’Daly published -
;3 anonymously the first' edition of Brian Merryman’s great Irish poem

P “The Midnight Court,”’ with the date MDCCC. on the imprint. Owing to-
ti the peculiar vein of humour which permeates this work of the Clare poet,

0’Daly was apparently shy about claiming credit for the publication. Is
this-instance of tampering with dates unique in the history of Irish
publishing ? S. UA. C.
